content:
# Leviticus Chapter 10 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 63r–63v | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus pp. 94–95

---

**1** Aaron's sons Nadab and Abihu each took his own fire-pan, put fire in it, laid incense on top, and offered unauthorized fire before the LORD — fire he had not commanded them. **2** Fire came out from before the LORD and consumed them, and they died before the LORD.

**3** Moses said to Aaron, "This is what the LORD meant when he said:

*'Among those who come near me I will show myself holy,*
*and before all the people I will be glorified.'"*

And Aaron was silent.

**4** Moses called Mishael and Elzaphan, the sons of Uzziel — Aaron's uncle — and said to them, "Come forward and carry your relatives away from the front of the sanctuary to a place outside the camp." **5** They came forward and carried them outside the camp in their tunics, as Moses had said.

**6** Moses said to Aaron and his sons Eleazar and Ithamar, "Do not uncover your heads and do not tear your clothes — or you will die, and anger will fall on the whole community. Your kinsmen, the whole house of Israel, may weep over the burning that the LORD has caused. **7** But you must not leave the entrance of the tent of meeting, or you will die, because the anointing oil of the LORD is upon you." And they did as Moses said.

**8** Then the LORD spoke to Aaron: **9** "You and your sons are not to drink wine or strong drink when you enter the tent of meeting, or you will die. This is a permanent statute throughout your generations. **10** You must distinguish between the holy and the common, between the unclean and the clean, **11** and you must teach the Israelites all the statutes the LORD has given them through Moses."

**12** Moses said to Aaron and his remaining sons Eleazar and Ithamar, "Take the grain offering left over from the LORD's fire offerings and eat it as unleavened bread beside the altar, because it is most holy. **13** Eat it in a holy place, because it is your portion and your sons' portion from the LORD's fire offerings — that is what I have been commanded. **14** The breast of the wave offering and the thigh of the contribution offering you may eat in any clean place, you and your sons and daughters with you, because they have been given to you and your children as your portion from the Israelites' peace offerings. **15** The thigh that is the contribution and the breast of the wave offering they shall bring with the fire offerings of fat, to be waved as a wave offering before the LORD. This is to be your and your children's permanent portion, as the LORD has commanded."

**16** Then Moses searched carefully for the sin offering goat — and found it had already been burned up. He was angry with Eleazar and Ithamar, Aaron's surviving sons, and said, **17** "Why did you not eat the sin offering in the holy place? It is most holy, and he gave it to you to remove the guilt of the community, to make atonement for them before the LORD. **18** Its blood was not brought inside the sanctuary — you absolutely should have eaten it in the sanctuary, as I commanded."

**19** Aaron spoke to Moses: "Look — today they offered their sin offering and their burnt offering before the LORD. And these things have happened to me. If I had eaten the sin offering today, would the LORD have approved?" **20** Moses heard this and approved.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**"Unauthorized fire" — the offense and its opacity.** Verse 1 names the transgression with four Hebrew words: *esh zarah asher lo tzivvah otam* — "strange/unauthorized fire which he had not commanded them." What makes it strange? The text does not say. Commentators have proposed incense offered at the wrong time, fire taken from outside the altar, the wrong kind of incense, offerings made while drunk (the alcohol prohibition in vv.8-11 follows immediately), or some combination. The deliberate vagueness is likely the point. Leviticus does not hand the reader a clear moral cause-and-effect diagram so that the outcome seems proportionate and therefore manageable. What the text establishes is not the specific nature of the fault but the fundamental principle: the mode of approach to the Holy One is not left to human invention. "Which he had not commanded them" — the command-structure is the very framework within which holiness operates. Nadab and Abihu approached as if the details were theirs to improvise. They were not.

**The same fire — the reversal of chapter 9.** The final verse of Leviticus 9 recorded: "Fire came out from before the LORD and consumed the burnt offering and the fat portions on the altar." Verse 2 of chapter 10 opens with the identical phrase: "Fire came out from before the LORD and consumed them." The verbal echo is precise and brutal. The fire of divine acceptance has become the fire of divine judgment — and the difference between the two firings is not the nature of the divine fire but the nature of what it meets. In chapter 9, the fire meets a correctly ordered offering; in chapter 10, it meets unauthorized presumption. The LORD's fire is not intrinsically either welcoming or destructive; it is the consuming holiness of God, and what it does to what it touches depends on whether what it touches has been prepared to receive it. This is Leviticus's terrifying logic: the same holiness that saves also destroys, depending on the terms of approach.

**Moses' quotation — "among those who come near me."** Verse 3 is one of the most compressed theological statements in the Old Testament. Moses quotes a prior divine saying that does not appear verbatim anywhere else in the Pentateuch in this form. "Among those who come near me I will show myself holy, and before all the people I will be glorified." The divine self-disclosure that all of Leviticus 8-9 has been building toward — the glory-appearance, the fire from heaven — comes at a price. Those who draw near to the Holy One (*bi-qerovai*) are the priests, and it is precisely their proximity that makes them the site of divine self-manifestation. The high stakes of priestly nearness are not merely ceremonial; they are ontological. The priest who draws near to what is sacred cannot do so casually, because the nearness itself is the place where holiness becomes visible — for good or for ill.

**Aaron's silence — one of Scripture's great silences.** Three Hebrew words: *va-yiddom Aharon* — "and Aaron was silent." His two eldest sons have just died by divine fire. Moses has explained it theologically. And Aaron does not speak. This silence has generated more commentary than almost any other verse in Leviticus. Is it obedience? Shock? Submission? Grief beyond words? The Talmud reads it as a form of righteous acquiescence — Aaron accepts the divine verdict without protest, and for this he is rewarded (the subsequent divine speech in vv.8-11 is addressed to Aaron alone, not through Moses). Elie Wiesel read it as the silence of a father whose grief is too large for language. However the silence is read, its weight is undeniable. The man who had been Aaron's mouthpiece (Moses spoke and Aaron translated) is now left wordless before an act of God he cannot question, cannot reverse, and — in the immediate moment — cannot even mourn. The prohibition on mourning in vv.6-7 seals the silence with ritual law: Aaron must not tear his clothes or dishevel his hair, because the anointing oil is upon him. The priest's body belongs to his office even in catastrophe. The silence is not merely emotional; it is professional, liturgical, and crushing.

**"Do not uncover your heads and do not tear your clothes."** The mourning prohibition in verses 6-7 is among the most humanly difficult commands in the Torah. Aaron and his surviving sons are forbidden the normal rituals of grief — loosening the hair (*et rosheikhem al tipra'u*) and tearing the garment (*u-vigdeikhem lo tiqra'u*) — under threat of death. The reason given is double: personal safety ("or you will die") and communal responsibility ("anger will fall on the whole community"). The logic seems to be that the priests, having been set apart by the anointing oil, cannot re-enter the realm of the dead and the unclean through the rituals of mourning without compromising their consecrated status at the very moment the sanctuary has been violated. The community can mourn. The priesthood cannot — not because grief is wrong, but because the office takes precedence over the man inside it. This is priestly vocation pressed to its most severe expression: the priest's body is not entirely his own.

**The alcohol prohibition — the interpretive hinge.** Scholars have long noted that the alcohol prohibition in verses 8-11 follows the Nadab-Abihu incident without transition or explanation, and several ancient interpreters drew the inference directly: Nadab and Abihu were drunk when they offered the unauthorized fire. The rabbis developed this as a majority opinion. Whether or not it is textually provable (and the text deliberately withholds a clear answer), the juxtaposition is hermeneutically significant. The prohibitions are immediately followed by the stated purpose: "to distinguish between the holy and the common, between the unclean and the clean, and to teach the Israelites all the statutes." The priest's sober judgment is the instrument by which Israel learns the categories that structure their entire existence before God. A priest who cannot distinguish, who blurs the boundaries, does not merely fail personally; he fails the community's entire orientation to the sacred. The phrase "you will die" is repeated from verse 2, drawing the legal prohibition into the narrative logic of the Nadab-Abihu tragedy: the stakes are not ceremonial. They are mortal.

**The holy portions and who eats them.** Verses 12-15 resume the instructions about eating the priestly portions that were given in chapter 8-9. The grain offering is most holy and must be eaten by the priests only, in a holy place, beside the altar. The wave breast and heave thigh — lower-grade holy than the sin-offering portions — may be eaten by the priests and their families in any clean place. The structure of the holy portions reflects a graduated sanctity: the closer to the altar, the more restricted the access. This eating is not merely a practical provision for the priests' livelihood; it is a participation in the sacrifice itself. The priest who eats the holy portion is, in the logic of Leviticus, completing the sacrifice — the offering goes up to God in smoke, and what remains comes down to the priest as a sacred meal. The priest's table is the altar's extension. The consumption is the completion.

**Moses' anger about the uneaten sin offering.** The chapter's final scene is a remarkable display of human conflict within the sanctuary system. Moses discovers that the sin offering goat — the *se'ir ha-chatat* — has been burned up entirely rather than eaten by the priests as prescribed (compare the sin offering instruction of Leviticus 6:24-26). He is angry (*va-yiqtsof*), and his anger is theologically grounded: the priest's eating of the sin offering is not optional. It is the mechanism by which the priest removes the guilt from the community. To burn it up without eating it is to leave the atonement incomplete. The question Moses presses — "Why did you not eat it?" — is not a bureaucratic complaint about procedure. It is a question about whether the community's forgiveness has been properly enacted.

**Aaron's defense — "these things have happened to me."** Verse 19 is Aaron's only speech in the entire chapter, and it is one of the most theologically charged moments in Leviticus. Literally: "Today they offered their sin offering and their burnt offering before the LORD, and things like these happened to me — if I had eaten the sin offering today, would it have been good in the LORD's eyes?" Aaron's reasoning is disputed. The most natural reading is pastoral and existential: on a day when God has consumed his two sons, on a day when his grief has been suppressed by priestly law, Aaron could not in good conscience eat the most holy food of the sin offering. His interior state was not "clean" in the sense required for eating the most holy portion. The sin offering eaten by a priest in mourning — by a man whose sons died today by the fire of the LORD — would not be a clean transaction. Moses hears this and approves (*va-yitav be-einav* — "it was good in his eyes"). The great lawgiver accepts a pastoral deviation from the rule on the grounds of the day's overwhelming circumstances. Aaron's defense wins. Leviticus, the book of precision and regulation, ends its most terrible chapter with an act of pastoral equity — a recognition that the law's purpose is the community's wellbeing before the LORD, and that on this particular day, Aaron's silence and Aaron's grief were their own kind of holiness.

**LXX (Vaticanus) observations — pages 94–95.** Codex Vaticanus presents Leviticus 10 across pages 94 and 95 (Vat.gr.1209/0098 and /0099) in three columns of careful uncial script. Page 94 carries the text from the end of chapter 9 into chapter 10, and page 95 concludes chapter 10 and begins chapter 11, with the chapter marker "P CAP XXXVII" (i.e., LXX chapter 37 = MT chapter 11) visible at the lower right margin of page 95 — indicating the LXX chapter enumeration differs from the modern Hebrew-based numbering.

At verse 1, the LXX renders *esh zarah* as *πῦρ ἀλλότριον* — "alien/foreign fire" (*allotrios*, belonging to another, foreign to this context). The Greek *allotrios* is sharper than "strange"; it denotes something that belongs to a different domain entirely, that has no place here. This strengthens the offense: the fire Nadab and Abihu offered was not merely irregular — it was ontologically out of place, fire from outside the sacred order that had no business crossing into the holy sphere.

At verse 2, the LXX uses *κατέφαγεν αὐτούς* — "consumed/devoured them" — with the verb *kataphagō*, which can mean to eat down, to swallow up completely. The same verb appears in the LXX of Exodus 24:17, where the appearance of the LORD's glory on Mount Sinai is "like consuming/devouring fire." The divine fire of chapter 9 (acceptance) and the divine fire of chapter 10 (judgment) are rendered with the same Greek verb — making the verbal echo between the two chapters even more pronounced in the Greek than in the Hebrew.

At verse 3, the LXX renders the divine saying as: *Ἐν τοῖς ἐγγίζουσίν μοι ἁγιασθήσομαι καὶ ἐν πάσῃ τῇ συναγωγῇ δοξασθήσομαι* — "In those who draw near to me I will be sanctified, and in all the congregation I will be glorified." The Greek *hagiasō* (future passive: "I will be sanctified/shown holy") renders the Hebrew *eqqadesh* perfectly. But notice *synagōgē* for the Hebrew *'am* (people): the LXX uses "congregation/assembly" rather than simply "people," suggesting a more specifically cultic context — this is not about the nation in a general sense but about the assembled worshiping community. The glory manifests in the liturgical assembly.

At verse 3, the LXX also records Aaron's silence: *καὶ κατενύχθη Ααρων* — not simply "Aaron was silent" (*va-yiddom*) but "Aaron was pierced/stunned" (*katenugō* — to be struck numb, to be dumbfounded). The Septuagint's Greek captures not merely the behavioral fact of silence but its inner quality: Aaron was not composed; he was devastated into speechlessness. The Greek verb implies a wound. The silence is not stoicism; it is paralysis.

At verse 6, the LXX's prohibition "do not uncover your heads" reads *τὴν κεφαλὴν ὑμῶν οὐκ ἀποκαλύψετε* — "you shall not uncover your head" — using the future indicative as an imperative, a characteristic LXX construction. More notably, at verse 7, "the anointing oil of the LORD is upon you" becomes *τὸ ἔλαιον τῆς χρίσεως τὸ ἐπ' ὑμῖν* — "the oil of anointing, that which is upon you." The Greek *chriseos* (of anointing, from *chriō*) is the root from which *Christos* ("the Anointed One") derives. In Vaticanus, the phrase marks Aaron and his sons as those upon whom the anointing rests — a christological echo that early Christian readers of the LXX would have heard in a passage about the terrible cost of priestly nearness to God.

---

*PNEUMA — translated from Leningrad Codex (B19A) folios 63r–63v, with reference to Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) pages 94–95 (LXX).*
